standard_description: Find the area of a rectangle with fractional side lengths by tiling it with unit squares of the appropriate unit fraction side lengths, and show that the area is the same as would be found by multiplying the side lengths. Multiply fractional side lengths to find areas of rectangles, and represent fraction products as rectangular areas.
subject: Math

problems.text:
Task
Chavone is remodeling her bathroom.  She plans to cover the bathroom floor with tiles that are each 1 square foot.  Her bathroom is $5\frac12$ feet wide and $8\frac14$  feet long.
How many tiles will she need to cover the floor? Give an exact answer that includes the fractions of a tile she will need. Each unit square has been broken into sixteenths to allow precise measurements. Use the figure below to illustrate your answer.
###IMAGE0###

Task
Part 1:
There are two design proposals for a new rectangular park in town.
In design one, $\frac{3}{4}$ of the area of the park is going to be a rectangular grass area and $\frac{1}{2}$ of the grass area will be a rectangular soccer field.
In design two, only $\frac{1}{2}$ of the park is going to be a rectangular grass area and $\frac{3}{4}$ of the grass area will be a rectangular soccer field.
Which design (one or two) will have a bigger soccer field?  Explain your answer. Draw a diagram that can be used to compare the size of the soccer field in the two designs.  Label the values $\frac{1}{2}$ and $\frac{3}{4}$ on the diagram.
Part 2:
Presley and Julia are cutting 1 ft. square poster board to make a sign for the new park. Presley cut her poster so that the length of the top and bottom are $\frac{1}{2}$ ft and the length of the sides are $\frac{3}{4}$ ft. Julia cut her poster so that the lengths of the top and bottom are $\frac{3}{4}$ ft and the length of the sides are $\frac{1}{2}$ ft.
Draw a diagram of each poster board.  Label the values on the diagram.
How are their poster boards similar and different?  Justify your reasoning.

Task
Part 1:
There are two design proposals for a new rectangular park in town.
In design one, $\frac{3}{4}$ of the area of the park is going to be a rectangular grass area and $\frac{1}{2}$ of the grass area will be a rectangular soccer field.
In design two, only $\frac{1}{2}$ of the park is going to be a rectangular grass area and $\frac{3}{4}$ of the grass area will be a rectangular soccer field.
Which design (one or two) will have a bigger soccer field?  Explain your answer. Draw a diagram that can be used to compare the size of the soccer field in the two designs.  Label the values $\frac{1}{2}$ and $\frac{3}{4}$ on the diagram.
Part 2:
Presley and Julia are cutting 1 ft. square poster board to make a sign for the new park. Presley cut her poster so that the length of the top and bottom are $\frac{1}{2}$ ft and the length of the sides are $\frac{3}{4}$ ft. Julia cut her poster so that the lengths of the top and bottom are $\frac{3}{4}$ ft and the length of the sides are $\frac{1}{2}$ ft.
Draw a diagram of each poster board.  Label the values on the diagram.
How are their poster boards similar and different?  Justify your reasoning.

Problem 2
Pre-unit
Write a multiplication equation shown by the shaded region of the diagram.
###IMAGE0###
What is the value of \(\frac{7}{10} \times \frac{5}{10}\)? Use the grid if it is helpful.
###IMAGE1###

khan_exercises.title: Represent fraction multiplication with visuals
khan_exercises.description: Represent fraction multiplication with area models, number lines, and tape diagrams

items.question_content:
The area of the entire figure below is $1$ square unit.  

[[☃ image 1]]

**How can we calculate the area of the striped rectangle?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can calculate the area of the striped rectangle by multiplying its
$\purpleD{\text{width}} \times \blueD{\text{height}}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The striped rectangle is $\purpleD{\dfrac19}$ of a unit wide and $\blueD{\dfrac1{3}}$ of a unit high.
We can calculate the area of the striped rectangle with the expression $\purpleD{\dfrac19} \times \blueD{\dfrac13}$.

options.alt: A square that has been split into 27 parts, set in 3 rows of 9 parts each. In the top row, the leftmost part  is striped to show that it is shaded both blue and purple and the other 8 parts have been shaded blue. In the middle and bottom rows, the leftmost parts have been shaded purple and the other 16 parts are unshaded.

items.question_content:
The area of the entire figure below is $1$ square unit.  

[[☃ image 1]]

**How can we calculate the area of the striped rectangle?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can calculate the area of the striped rectangle by multiplying its
$\purpleE{\text{width}} \times \blueE{\text{height}}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The striped rectangle is $\purpleE{\dfrac19}$ of a unit wide and $\blueE{\dfrac1{3}}$ of a unit high.
We can calculate the area of the striped rectangle with the expression $\purpleE{\dfrac19} \times \blueE{\dfrac13}$.

options.alt: A square that has been split into 27 parts, set in 3 rows of 9 parts each. In the top row, the leftmost part  is striped to show that it is shaded both blue and purple and the other 8 parts have been shaded blue. In the middle and bottom rows, the leftmost parts have been shaded purple and the other 16 parts are unshaded. Along the top of the  square, the columns are labeled one-ninth  Along the left side of the square the rows are labeled one-third.

items.question_content:
The area of the entire figure below is $1$ square unit.
 
[[☃ image 1]]

**How can we describe the area of the striped rectangle?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We can calculate the area of the striped rectangle by multiplying its
$\purpleE{\text{width}} \times \blueE{\text{height}}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

The striped rectangle is $\purpleE{\dfrac4{10}}$ of a unit wide and $\blueE{\dfrac23}$ of a unit high.

We can calculate the area of the striped rectangle with the expression $\purpleE{\dfrac4{10}} \times \blueE{\dfrac23}$.

The expression $\purpleE{\dfrac4{10}} \times \blueE{\dfrac23}$ is another way of saying $\purpleE{\dfrac4{10}}$ of $\blueE{\dfrac23}$.
The area of the striped rectangle is $\dfrac{4}{10}$ of $\dfrac{2}{3}$.

options.alt: A square that has been split into 30 parts, set in 3 rows of 10 parts each. In the top 2 rows, the leftmost 4 parts are striped to show that they are shaded both blue and purple and the other 12 parts have been shaded blue. In the bottom row, the leftmost parts have been shaded purple and the other 6 parts are unshaded. Along the top of the  square, the columns are labeled one-tenth  Along the left side of the square the rows are labeled one-third.

items.question_content:
The area of each square below is $1$ square unit.

[[☃ image 1]]

**How can we calculate the area of the striped region?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
If we push both squares together, the striped region will form a rectangle. 

We can calculate the area of the striped rectangle by multiplying its
$\purpleE{\text{width}} \times \blueE{\text{height}}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 3]]

The striped rectangle is $\purpleE{\dfrac75}$ of a unit wide and $\blueE{\dfrac12}$ of a unit high.
We can calculate the area of the striped region with the expression $\purpleE{\dfrac75} \times \blueE{\dfrac12}$.

options.alt: Two equal-sized squares side by side. Each square  has been split into 10 parts, set in 2 rows of 5 parts each. In the first square, the parts in the bottom row have been shaded purple and the parts in the top row are striped to show they are shaded both blue and purple. In the second square, the first two parts in the top row are striped to show they are shaded both blue and purple and the last three parts in the top row have been shaded blue. The first two parts in the bottom row have been shaded purple and the last three parts in the bottom row are unshaded. Along the top of each square, the columns are labeled one-fifth  Along the left side of the squares the rows are labeled one-half.

items.question_content:
The area of each square below is $1$ square unit.

[[☃ image 1]]

**How can we describe the area of the striped region?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
If we push both squares together, the striped region will form a rectangle.

We can calculate the area of the striped rectangle by multiplying its
$\purpleE{\text{width}} \times \blueE{\text{height}}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 3]]

The striped rectangle is $\purpleE{\dfrac54}$ of a unit wide and $\blueE{\dfrac23}$ of a unit high.

We can calculate the area of the striped region with the expression $\purpleE{\dfrac54} \times \blueE{\dfrac23}$.

The expression $\purpleE{\dfrac54} \times \blueE{\dfrac23}$ is another way of saying $\purpleE{\dfrac54}$ of $\blueE{\dfrac23}$.
The area of the striped rectangle is $\dfrac{5}{4}$ of $\dfrac{2}{3}$.

options.alt: Two equal-sized squares side by side. Each square  has been split into 12 parts, set in 3 rows of 4 parts each. In the first square, the parts in the bottom row have been shaded purple and the parts in the middle and top rows are striped to show they are shaded both blue and purple. In the second square, the leftmost parts in the top and middle rows are striped to show they are shaded both blue and purple and the last three parts in each of these rows have been shaded blue. The leftmost part in the bottom row have been shaded purple and the last three parts in the bottom row are unshaded. Along the top of each square, the columns are labeled one-fourth.  Along the left side of the squares the rows are labeled one-third.

items.question_content:
The tape diagram below represents fraction multiplication.

  [[☃ image 2]]

**Which expression represents the image?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The image shows that we are finding a fraction of another fraction.

We can use multiplication to show that we are making equal-sized groups, even when the size is a fraction!

The *whole* has $7$ equal pieces, with $6$ pieces shaded.

[[☃ image 1]]


The shaded parts represent $\dfrac{6}{7}$ of $1$ whole.

The *shaded parts* have $6$ equal pieces, with $3$ pieces marked.

[[☃ image 1]]

The selected parts represent $\dfrac{3}{6}$ of $\dfrac{6}{7}$.
$\dfrac{3}{6} \times \dfrac{6}{7}$ is another way of saying $\dfrac{3}{6}$ of $\dfrac{6}{7}$.
We can represent the image with the expression $\dfrac{3}{6}\times\dfrac{6}{7}$.

options.alt: A tape diagram labeled 1 with 6 of 7 equal parts shaded, each labeled 1 seventh. A brace labeled question mark spans 3 of the parts.

items.question_content:
The tape diagram below represents fraction multiplication.
  
[[☃ image 2]]

**Which description represents the image?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The image shows that we are finding a fraction of another fraction.

We can use multiplication to show that we are making equal-sized groups, even when the size is a fraction!

Each *whole* has $2$ equal pieces, and there are $9$ pieces shaded.


[[☃ image 1]]

The shaded parts represent $\dfrac{9}{2}$ of $1$ whole.

The *shaded parts* have $9$ equal pieces, with $6$ pieces marked.


[[☃ image 1]]

The selected parts represent $\dfrac{6}{9}$ of $\dfrac{9}{2}$.
We can represent the image with the description $\dfrac{6}{9}$ of $\dfrac{9}{2}$.

options.alt: A tape diagram with 10 equal parts each labeled 1 half. Braces labeled 1 span every group of 2 sections. 9 parts are shaded. A brace labeled question mark spans 6 of the parts.

items.question_content:
The tape diagrams below represent fraction multiplication.

[[☃ image 3]]

[[☃ image 4]]

**Which description represents the image?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The image shows that we are finding a fraction of another fraction.

We can use multiplication to show that we are making equal-sized groups, even when the size is a fraction!

The *whole* has $4$ equal pieces, with $3$ pieces shaded.


[[☃ image 1]]

The shaded parts represent $\dfrac{3}{4}$ of $1$ whole.

Then we split each *fourth* in $3$ equal pieces.

[[☃ image 1]]

Now, the *shaded parts* have $9$ equal pieces, with $4$ pieces marked.


[[☃ image 1]]

The selected parts represent $\dfrac{4}{9}$ of $\dfrac{3}{4}$.
We can represent the image with the description $\dfrac{4}{9}$ of $\dfrac{3}{4}$.

options.alt: A tape diagram labeled 1 with 3 of 4 equal parts labeled 1 fourth shaded.
The same tape diagram as above, with each part split in 3 equal parts for a total of 12 equal parts. A brace labeled question mark spans 4 of the parts.

items.question_content:
The tape diagrams below represent fraction multiplication.

[[☃ image 3]]

[[☃ image 4]]

**Which expression represents the image?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The image shows that we are finding a fraction of another fraction.

We can use multiplication to show that we are making equal-sized groups, even when the size is a fraction!

Each *whole* has $2$ equal pieces, and there are $3$ pieces shaded.



[[☃ image 1]]

The shaded parts represent $\dfrac{3}{2}$ of $1$ whole.

Then we split each *half* in $2$ equal pieces.


[[☃ image 1]]

Now, the *shaded parts* have $6$ equal pieces, with $5$ pieces marked.



[[☃ image 1]]

The selected parts represent $\dfrac{5}{6}$ of $\dfrac{3}{2}$.
$\dfrac{5}{6} \times \dfrac{3}{2}$ is another way of saying $\dfrac{5}{6}$ of $\dfrac{3}{2}$.
We can represent the image with the expression $\dfrac{5}{6} \times \dfrac{3}{2}$.

options.alt: Two rectangles, each split into 2 parts with a curved bracket above each rectangle labeled 1. Each part is labeled 1 half.  Three parts are shaded. One part is not shaded.

Two rectangles, each split into 2 parts. Each part is further split into 2 parts, so there are two wholes with 4 parts each. Five of the parts are shaded in a dark pink. One part is shaded in light pink. Two parts are not shaded. A curved bracket across the parts shaded in dark pink is labeled question mark.

items.question_content:
The number line below represents fraction multiplication.
  
[[☃ image 2]]

**Which description represents the shaded length of the number line?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The image shows that we are finding a fraction of another fraction.

We can use multiplication to show that we are making equal-sized groups, even when the size is a fraction!

The *fraction bar* has a shaded length of $3$ halves.

[[☃ image 1]]

Each *half* has $5$ equal pieces, with $4$ pieces shaded on the number line.

[[☃ image 1]]

The shaded length on the number line represents $\dfrac{4}{5}$ of $3$ halves.
We can represent the shaded length with the description $\dfrac{4}{5}$ of $\dfrac{3}{2}$.

options.alt: A number line labeled 0 to 2 with minor tick marks every one-tenth unit and major tick marks every one-half unit. The major tick marks are labeled from left to right: zero, one-half, one, three-halves, and 2. The number line is shaded from the 0 tick mark to the three-halves tick mark. Above the number line, a fraction bar is divided into 20 equal parts. The first 4 parts, parts 6 through 9, and parts 11 through 14 are shaded in dark green. The first 4 dark green parts are between 0 and the fourth tick mark after 0, the next 4 dark green parts are between one-half and the fourth tick mark after one-half, and the last 4 dark green parts are between 1 and the fourth tick mark after 1. The fifth, tenth, and fifteenth parts are shaded in light green. The first light green part is between the fourth  tick mark after 0 and the one-half tick mark, the next light green part is between the fourth tick mark after one-half and the 1 tick mark, and the last light green part is  between the fourth tick mark after  1 and the three-halves tick mark. The parts 16 through 20 are unshaded.

items.question_content:
The number line below represents fraction multiplication.
  
[[☃ image 2]]

**Which expression represents the shaded length of the number line?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The image shows that we are finding a fraction of another fraction.

We can use multiplication to show that we are making equal-sized groups, even when the size is a fraction!

The *fraction bar* has a shaded length of $3$ fifths.



[[☃ image 1]]

Each *fifth* has $4$ equal pieces, with $3$ pieces shaded on the number line.



[[☃ image 1]]

The shaded length on the number line represents $\dfrac{3}{4}$ of $3$ fifths.
We can represent the shaded length with the expression $\dfrac{3}{4} \times \dfrac{3}{5}$.

options.alt: A fraction bar divided into 20 equal parts. The left 12 parts are shaded in a pattern: 3 dark parts, 1 light part, 3 dark parts, 1 light part, 3 dark parts, 1 light part. The right 8 sections of the fraction bar are not shaded. Below the fraction bar, there is a number line the same length as the fraction bar. A number line below the fraction bar is labeled 0 to 1 with minor tick marks every one-twentieth unit and major tick marks every one-fifth unit. Three lines move right across the number line. The first line moves right three tick marks from 0 to the tick mark before one-fifth. The second line moves right three tick marks from one-fifth to the tick mark before two-fifths. The third line moves right three tick marks from two-fifths to the tick mark before three-fifths.

items.question_content:
The number line below represents fraction multiplication.

  [[☃ image 2]]

**Which description represents the shaded length of the number line?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The image shows that we are finding a fraction of another fraction.

We can use multiplication to show that we are making equal-sized groups, even when the size is a fraction!

The *fraction bar* has a shaded length of $5$ sixths.

[[☃ image 1]]

Each *sixth* has $2$ equal pieces, for a total of $2\times 5 = 10$ shaded pieces. The number line shades the lengths of $5$ of those pieces.

[[☃ image 1]]

The shaded length on the number line represents $\dfrac{5}{10}$ of $5$ sixths.

$\dfrac{5}{10}$ is equivalent to $\dfrac{1}{2}$.

[[☃ image 1]]
We can represent the shaded length with the description $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of $\dfrac{5}{6}$.

options.alt: A number line labeled 0 to 1 with minor tick marks every one-twelfth unit and major tick marks every one-sixth unit. The major tick marks are labeled from left to right: zero, one-sixth, one-third, one-half, two-thirds, five-sixths, and one. The number line is shaded from the 0 tick mark to the first tick mark after one-third. Above the number line, a fraction bar is divided into 12 equal parts. The first 5 parts are shaded in dark blue. The dark blue parts are between 0 and the first tick mark after one-third on the number line. The next 5 parts are shaded in light blue. The light blue parts are between the first tick mark after one-third and five-sixths on the number line.

items.question_content:
The number line below represents fraction multiplication.
  
[[☃ image 2]]

**Which expression represents the shaded length of the number line?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The image shows that we are finding a fraction of another fraction.

We can use multiplication to show that we are making equal-sized groups, even when the size is a fraction!

The *fraction bar* has a shaded length of $5$ fourths.



[[☃ image 1]]

Each *fourth* has $3$ equal pieces, for a total of $3\times 5 = 15$ shaded pieces. The number line shades the lengths of $10$ of those pieces.



[[☃ image 1]]

The shaded length on the number line represents $\dfrac{10}{15}$ of $5$ fourths.

$\dfrac{10}{15}$ is equivalent to $\dfrac{2}{3}$.



[[☃ image 1]]
We can represent the shaded length with the expression $\dfrac{2}{3} \times \dfrac{5}{4}$.

options.alt: A number line labeled 0 to three-halves with minor tick marks every one-twelfth unit and major tick marks every one-fourth unit. The major tick marks are labeled from left to right: zero, one-fourth, one-half, three-quarters, one, five-fourths, and three-halves. The number line is shaded from the 0 tick mark to the first tick mark after three-fourths. Above the number line, a fraction bar is divided into 18 equal parts. The first 10 parts are shaded in dark purple. The dark purple parts are between 0 and the first tick mark after three-quarters on the number line. The next 5 parts are shaded in light purple. The light purple parts are between the first tick mark after three-quarters and five-fourths on the number line.

khan_exercises.title: Multiplying fractions with visuals
khan_exercises.description: Use area models and tape diagrams to multiply a fraction times a fraction.

items.question_content:
Use the number line below to help solve the multiplication problem.  

[[☃ image 1]]  

**What is $\dfrac{6}{7}$ of $\dfrac{2}{3}$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
First we find $2$ thirds on the number line.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can't split $2$ pieces into a whole number of sevenths, so we'll split every piece into $7$ equal parts instead.

[[☃ image 2]]

Then we shade $\dfrac{6}{7}$ of every third on the number line.

[[☃ image 1]]

There are $2\times 6 = 12$ parts shaded on the number line.

There are $7 \times 3 = 21$ parts per whole, so each part is $\dfrac{1}{21}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

We shaded $12$ of the $21$ equal parts on the number line, so $\dfrac{6}{7}$ of $\dfrac{2}{3}=\dfrac{12}{21}$.

We can also solve using multiplication.  

$\dfrac{6}{7}$ of $\dfrac23$ is another way of saying $\dfrac67 \times \dfrac23$.

We can rewrite $\dfrac{12}{21}$ as an equivalent fraction.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{12}{21}&=\dfrac{4 \times 3}{7 \times 3}\\\\
&=\dfrac{4}{7}\times\dfrac{3}{3}\\\\
&=\dfrac{4}{7}\times1\\\\
&=\dfrac{4}{7}
\end{align}$
$\dfrac67$ of $\dfrac23$ is $\dfrac47$ .

options.alt: A number line starting at 0 has minor tick marks every one twenty-first unit and major tick marks every one-third unit. The major tick marks are labeled from left to right: zero, one-third, and two-thirds, one. The number line is shaded from the 0 tick mark to the sixth tick mark after 0 and from the one-third tick mark to the sixth tick mark after one-third.  Above the number line, a fraction bar is divided into 3 equal parts. Each part is further divided into 7 smaller parts for a total of 21 smaller parts. The first 6 parts and parts 8 through 13 are shaded in dark green. The dark green parts are between 0 and the sixth tick mark after 0 and between one-third and the sixth tick mark after one-third on the number line. Seventh and fourteenth parts are shaded in light green. The light green parts are between the sixth tick mark after 0 and one-third tick mark and between the sixth tick mark after one-third and two-thirds tick mark on the number line.

items.question_content:
Use the number line below to help solve the multiplication problem.

[[☃ image 1]]  

**What is $\dfrac{3}{4}$ of $\dfrac{2}{5}$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
First we find $2$ fifths on the number line.

[[☃ image 1]]

Let's split every piece into $4$ equal parts.

[[☃ image 2]]

Then we shade $\dfrac{3}{4}$ of every fifth on the number line.


[[☃ image 1]]

There are $3\times 2 = 6$ parts shaded on the number line.

There are $4 \times 5 = 20$ parts per whole, so each part is $\dfrac{1}{20}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

We shaded $6$ of the $20$ equal parts on the number line, so $\dfrac{3}{4}$ of $\dfrac{2}{5}=\dfrac{6}{20}$.

We can also solve using multiplication.  

$\dfrac{3}{4}$ of $\dfrac{2}{5}$is another way of saying $\dfrac{3}{4} \times \dfrac{2}{5}$.

We can rewrite $\dfrac{6}{20}$ as an equivalent fraction.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{6}{20}&=\dfrac{3 \times 2}{10 \times 2}\\\\
&=\dfrac{3}{10}\times\dfrac{2}{2}\\\\
&=\dfrac{3}{10}\times1\\\\
&=\dfrac{3}{10}
\end{align}$
$\dfrac34$ of $\dfrac25$ is $\dfrac3{10}$ .

items.question_content:
Use the number line below to help solve the multiplication problem.

[[☃ image 1]]  

**What is $\dfrac{3}{8}$ of $\dfrac{1}{4}$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
First we find $1$ fourth on the number line.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can't split $1$ piece into a whole number of eighths, so we'll split every piece into $8$ equal parts instead.

[[☃ image 2]]

Then we shade $\dfrac{3}{8}$ of every fourth on the number line.


[[☃ image 1]]

There are $3\times 1 = 3$ parts shaded on the number line.

There are $8 \times 4 = 32$ parts per whole, so each part is $\dfrac{1}{32}$.

We shaded $3$ of the $32$ equal parts on the number line, so $\dfrac{3}{8}$ of $\dfrac{1}{4}=\dfrac{3}{32}$.

We can also solve using multiplication.  

$\dfrac{3}{8}$ of $\dfrac14$is another way of saying $\dfrac{3}{8} \times \dfrac14$.
$\dfrac38$ of $\dfrac14$ is $\dfrac3{32}$ .

options.alt: A number line starting at 0 to 1 with minor tick marks every one thirty-second unit and major tick marks every one-fourth unit. The major tick marks are labeled from left to right: zero, one-fourth, one-half, three-fourths, and 1. The number line is shaded from the 0 tick mark to the third tick mark after 0. Above the number line, a fraction bar is divided into 4 equal parts. Each part is further divided into 8 smaller parts for a total of 32 smaller parts. The first 3 parts are shaded in dark purple. The dark purple parts are between 0 and the third tick mark after 0 on the number line. The next 5 parts are shaded in light purple. The light purple parts are between the third tick mark after 0 and one-fourth tick mark on the number line.

items.question_content:
Use the number line below to help solve the multiplication problem.

[[☃ image 1]]  

**What is $\dfrac56$ of $\dfrac12$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
First we find $1$ half on the number line.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can't split $1$ piece into a whole number of sixths, so we'll split every piece into $6$ equal parts instead.


[[☃ image 2]]

Then we shade $\dfrac{5}{6}$ of every half on the number line.


[[☃ image 1]]

There are $1\times 5 = 5$ parts shaded on the number line.

There are $6 \times 2 = 12$ parts per whole, so each part is $\dfrac{1}{12}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

We shaded $5$ of the $12$ equal parts on the number line, so $\dfrac{5}{6}$ of $\dfrac{1}{2}=\dfrac{5}{12}$.

We can also solve using multiplication.  

$\dfrac56$ of $\dfrac12$is another way of saying $\dfrac56 \times \dfrac12$.
$\dfrac56$ of $\dfrac12$ is $\dfrac5{12}$ .

options.alt: A number line starting at 0 to 1 with minor tick marks every one twelfth unit and major tick marks every one-half unit. The major tick marks are labeled from left to right: zero, one-half, and 1. The number line is shaded from the 0 tick mark to the fifth tick mark after 0.  Above the number line, a fraction bar is divided into 2 equal parts. Each part is further divided into 6 smaller parts for a total of 12 smaller parts. The first 5 parts are shaded in dark blue. The dark blue parts are between 0 and the fifth tick mark after 0 on the number line. The sixth part is shaded in light blue. The light blue part is between the fifth tick mark after 0 and one-half tick mark on the number line.

items.question_content:
Use the number line below to help solve the multiplication problem.

[[☃ image 1]]  

**What is $\dfrac23$ of $\dfrac9{10}$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
First we find $9$ tenths on the number line.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can split $9$ pieces into a whole number of thirds

[[☃ image 2]]

Every $\dfrac{3}{10}$ equals $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of $\dfrac{9}{10}$.

Then we shade $2$ of the thirds.
We shaded $6$ of the $10$ equal parts on the number line, so $\dfrac{2}{3}$ of $\dfrac{9}{10}$ is $\dfrac{6}{10}$.

We can also solve using multiplication.  

$\dfrac23$ of $\dfrac9{10}$is another way of saying $\dfrac23 \times \dfrac9{10}$.

We can rewrite $\dfrac{18}{30}$ as an equivalent fraction.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{18}{30}&=\dfrac{3 \times 6}{5 \times 6}\\\\
&=\dfrac{3}{5}\times\dfrac{6}{6}\\\\
&=\dfrac{3}{5}\times1\\\\
&=\dfrac{3}{5}
\end{align}$
$\dfrac23$ of $\dfrac9{10}$ is $\dfrac3{5}$ .

options.alt: A number line starting at 0 to 1 with ick marks every one-tenth unit. The tick marks are labeled from left to right: one-tenth, two-tenths, three-tenths, four-tenths, five-tenths, six-tenths, seven-tenths, eight-tenths, nine-tenths, and 1. The number line is shaded from the 0 tick mark to the six-tenths tick mark. Above the number line, a fraction bar is divided into 10 equal parts. The first 6 parts are shaded in dark purple. The dark blue parts are between 0 and six-tenths tick marks on the number line. The next 3 parts are shaded in light purple. The light purple parts are between the sixth-tenths and nine-tenths tick marks on the number line.

hints:

Only $\blueE{\dfrac16}$ of the $\purpleE{\dfrac13}$ of the unit is striped.

What is $\blueE{\dfrac16}$ of $\purpleE{\dfrac13}$?


We can see that $1$ of the $18$ equal parts from the whole unit has stripes.  

$\blueE{\dfrac16}$ of $\purpleE{\dfrac13}=\dfrac1{18}$

We can also solve with multiplication.

$\blueE{\dfrac16}$ of $\purpleE{\dfrac13}$ is another way of saying $\blueE{\dfrac16} \times \purpleE{\dfrac13}$.

So we can calculate the area of the striped rectangle by multiplying its
$\blueE{\text{height}} \times \purpleE{\text{width}}$.
The area of the striped rectangle is $\dfrac{1}{18}$ of a square unit.

**What is $\dfrac{3}{4}$ of $\dfrac{2}{5}$?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] of a square unit
hints:
The area of the entire figure is $1$ square unit.

[[☃ image 1]]

The striped rectangle is $\purpleE{\dfrac34}$ of a unit wide.

Only $\blueE{\dfrac25}$ of the $\purpleE{\dfrac34}$ of the unit is striped.

What is $\blueE{\dfrac25}$ of $\purpleE{\dfrac34}$?


We can see that $6$ of the $20$ equal parts from the whole unit has stripes.  

$\blueE{\dfrac25}$ of $\purpleE{\dfrac34}=\dfrac6{20}$

We can also solve with multiplication.

$\blueE{\dfrac25}$ of $\purpleE{\dfrac34}$ is another way of saying $\blueE{\dfrac25} \times \purpleE{\dfrac34}$.

So we can calculate the area of the striped rectangle by multiplying its
$\blueE{\text{height}} \times \purpleE{\text{width}}$.

We can rewrite $\dfrac{6}{20}$ as an equivalent fraction.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{6}{20}&=\dfrac{3 \times 2}{10 \times 2}\\\\
&=\dfrac{3}{10}\times\dfrac{2}{2}\\\\
&=\dfrac{3}{10}\times1\\\\
&=\dfrac{3}{10}
\end{align}$
The area of the striped rectangle is $\dfrac{3}{10}$ of a square unit.

$\blueE{\dfrac34}$ of $\purpleE{\dfrac56}=\dfrac{15}{24}$

We can also solve with multiplication.

$\blueE{\dfrac34}$ of $\purpleE{\dfrac56}$ is another way of saying $\blueE{\dfrac34} \times \purpleE{\dfrac56}$.

So we can calculate the area of the striped rectangle by multiplying its
$\blueE{\text{height}} \times \purpleE{\text{width}}$.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{15}{24}&=\dfrac{5 \times 3}{8 \times 3}\\\\
&=\dfrac{5}{8}\times\dfrac{3}{3}\\\\
&=\dfrac{5}{8}\times1\\\\
&=\dfrac{5}{8}
\end{align}$

hints:

The striped rectangle is $\purpleE{\dfrac32}$ of a unit wide.

Only $\blueE{\dfrac36}$ of the $\purpleE{\dfrac32}$ of the unit is striped.

What is $\blueE{\dfrac36}$ of $\purpleD{\dfrac32}$?

We can see that $9$ equal parts are striped. There are $12$ equal parts in one whole unit.

$\blueE{\dfrac36}$ of $\purpleE{\dfrac32}=\dfrac{9}{12}$

We can also solve with multiplication.

$\blueE{\dfrac36}$ of $\purpleE{\dfrac32}$ is another way of saying $\blueE{\dfrac36} \times \purpleE{\dfrac32}$.

So we can calculate the area of the striped rectangle by multiplying its
$\blueE{\text{height}} \times \purpleE{\text{width}}$.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{9}{12}&=\dfrac{3 \times 3}{4 \times 3}\\\\
&=\dfrac{3}{4}\times\dfrac{3}{3}\\\\
&=\dfrac{3}{4}\times1\\\\
&=\dfrac{3}{4}
\end{align}$

items.question_content:
Use the tape diagram below to help solve the multiplication problem.


[[☃ image 1]]  

**What is $\dfrac12$ of $\dfrac67?$**  
  
[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
The *whole* has $7$ equal pieces, with $6$ pieces shaded.

[[☃ image 1]]

The shaded parts represent $\dfrac{6}{7}$ of $1$ whole.

The *shaded parts* have $6$ equal pieces, with $3$ pieces marked.


[[☃ image 1]]

The selected parts represent $\dfrac{3}{6}$ of $\dfrac{6}{7}$.

$\dfrac{3}{6}$ is equivalent to $\dfrac{1}{2}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

So the selected parts represent $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of $\dfrac{6}{7}$.

What is $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of $\dfrac{6}{7}$?

The whole is divided into $7$ equal parts and $3$ are shaded.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\dfrac{1}{2}$ of $\dfrac{6}{7}=\dfrac37$

We can also solve using multiplication.  

$\dfrac{1}{2}$ of $\dfrac{6}{7}$ is another way of saying $\dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{6}{7}$.

We can rewrite $\dfrac{6}{14}$ as an equivalent fraction.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{6}{14}&=\dfrac{3 \times 2}{7 \times 2}\\\\
&=\dfrac{3}{7}\times\dfrac{2}{2}\\\\
&=\dfrac{3}{7}\times1\\\\
&=\dfrac{3}{7}
\end{align}$
$\dfrac12$ of $\dfrac67$ is $\dfrac37$ .

options.alt: A tape diagram has been divided into seven equal parts. 3 parts are darkly shaded, 3 parts are lightly shaded, and 1 part is not shaded. Across the bar, a curved bracket is labeled 1. Below the 3 darkly shaded parts, a curved bracket is labeled with a question mark.

items.question_content:
Use the tape diagram below to help solve the multiplication problem.

[[☃ image 1]]

  **What is $\dfrac12$ of $\dfrac89?$**    

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
The *whole* has $9$ equal pieces, with $8$ pieces shaded.

[[☃ image 1]]

The shaded parts represent $\dfrac89$ of $1$ whole.

The *shaded parts* have $8$ equal pieces, with $4$ pieces marked.

[[☃ image 1]]

The selected parts represent $\dfrac48$ of $\dfrac89$.

$\dfrac48$ is equivalent to $\dfrac{1}{2}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

So the selected parts represent $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of $\dfrac89$.

What is $\dfrac12$ of $\dfrac89$?

The whole is divided into $9$ equal parts and $4$ are shaded.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\dfrac12$ of $\dfrac89=\dfrac49$

We can also solve using multiplication.  

$\dfrac12$ of $\dfrac89$ is another way of saying $\dfrac1 2\times \dfrac89$.

We can rewrite $\dfrac{8}{18}$ as an equivalent fraction.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{8}{18}&=\dfrac{4 \times 2}{9 \times 2}\\\\
&=\dfrac49\times\dfrac22\\\\
&=\dfrac49\times1\\\\
&=\dfrac49
\end{align}$
$\dfrac12$ of $\dfrac89$ is $\dfrac49$ .

options.alt: A tape diagram has been divided into nine equal parts. 4 parts are darkly shaded, 4 parts are lightly shaded, and 1 part is not shaded. Across the bar, a curved bracket is labeled 1. Below the 4 darkly shaded parts, a curved bracket is labeled with a question mark.

items.question_content:
Use the tape diagram below to help solve the multiplication problem.


[[☃ image 1]]

  **What is $\dfrac23$ of $\dfrac38?$**    

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
The *whole* has $8$ equal pieces, with $3$ pieces shaded.

[[☃ image 1]]

The shaded parts represent $\dfrac38$ of $1$ whole.

The *shaded parts* have $3$ equal pieces, with $2$ pieces marked.


[[☃ image 1]]

The selected parts represent $\dfrac23$ of $\dfrac38$.

What is $\dfrac23$ of $\dfrac38$?

The whole is divided into $8$ equal parts and $2$ are shaded.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\dfrac23$ of $\dfrac38=\dfrac28$

We can also solve using multiplication.  

$\dfrac23$ of $\dfrac38$ is another way of saying $\dfrac23 \times \dfrac38$.

We can rewrite $\dfrac{6}{24}$ as an equivalent fraction.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{6}{24}&=\dfrac{1 \times 6}{4 \times 6}\\\\
&=\dfrac14\times\dfrac66\\\\
&=\dfrac14\times1\\\\
&=\dfrac14
\end{align}$
$\dfrac23$ of $\dfrac38$ is $\dfrac14$ .

 **What is $\dfrac58$ of $\dfrac45?$**    
 
[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 1]]

We can also solve using multiplication.  

$\dfrac58$ of $\dfrac45$ is another way of saying $\dfrac58\times \dfrac45$.

We can rewrite $\dfrac{20}{40}$ as an equivalent fraction.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{20}{40}&=\dfrac{1 \times 20}{2 \times 20}\\\\
&=\dfrac12\times\dfrac{20}{20}\\\\
&=\dfrac12\times1\\\\
&=\dfrac12
\end{align}$
$\dfrac58$ of $\dfrac45$ is $\dfrac12$ .
